refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-006 (Refined): The NGHE solution shall generate and maintain a real-time worksite model representing terrain elevation, static obstacles (rocks, structures, vegetation), dynamic hazards (personnel, other equipment), and work-zone boundaries, with sufficient fidelity to support autonomous path planning and operator situational awareness.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR was vague regarding fidelity requirements, update frequency, and scope of representable features. Refinement clarifies that the model must support both planning (path generation) and execution (real-time awareness) functions, and must distinguish static vs. dynamic content. High safety criticality demands explicit hazard representation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Acceptance test: (1) Worksite model generated within 5 minutes of mission start; (2) Terrain elevation accuracy ±0.5 m over 95% of mapped area; (3) Static obstacles detected and represented with ≥90% recall; (4) Dynamic hazards updated at ≥2 Hz; (5) Model accessible to planning and teleoperation functions via standardized interface.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain and obstacle data from onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU).
CR-NGHE-006.2: Fuse multi-sensor data into a unified 3D spatial representation.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Classify and label terrain, static obstacles, and dynamic hazards.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Maintain and update worksite model in real-time during mission execution.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Expose worksite model to planning, teleoperation, and safety services via standardized query/subscription interface.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized mapping service (SLAM-based) running on vehicle compute, publishing model updates via Telemetry interface to planning and teleoperation clients.
DP-B: Distributed sensor fusion: onboard vehicle processes LiDAR/camera locally; transmits raw sensor streams to remote mapping server; server maintains authoritative model and broadcasts updates.
DP-C: Hybrid edge-cloud: vehicle runs lightweight occupancy grid; cloud service performs full SLAM and semantic labeling; vehicle subscribes to refined model updates.
DP-D: Decentralized peer model: each vehicle maintains its own local map; models synchronized via peer-to-peer network; no central authority.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Centralized mapping service (SLAM-based) running on vehicle compute, publishing model updates via Telemetry interface to planning and teleoperation clients.

potential_sources: Coupling to vehicle compute capacity (must reserve CPU/GPU for SLAM).
Coupling to Telemetry interface bandwidth (model updates must fit within available telemetry rate).
Coupling to sensor suite (SLAM quality depends on LiDAR/camera calibration and placement).
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is decoupled from remote planning and teleoperation logic; model is published asynchronously. Coupling is primarily to vehicle hardware and network interface, which are acceptable dependencies. DP-B and DP-C introduce latency and cloud dependency; DP-D lacks authority for safety-critical hazard consensus. DP-A balances autonomy, latency, and safety.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Vehicle compute has sufficient CPU/GPU for real-time SLAM (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson or equivalent).
Assumption: LiDAR and camera are calibrated and mounted with known extrinsics.
Risk: SLAM may fail in featureless terrain or heavy dust/rain; fallback to dead-reckoning + manual operator input required.
Risk: Telemetry bandwidth may be insufficient for high-resolution model updates; compression or selective transmission needed.
Risk: Model initialization (first-time mapping) may take 30–60 seconds; mission planning must account for this latency.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Specify SLAM algorithm (e.g., ORB-SLAM2, LiDAR-based ICP) and compute platform in DP detail. Define model update rate (e.g., 10 Hz) and compression strategy (e.g., octree, voxel grid). Clarify fallback behavior if SLAM tracking is lost.

im_boundary.name: Vehicle
im_boundary.scope: The Vehicle module owns the onboard SLAM-based mapping service, including sensor acquisition (LiDAR, camera), data fusion, spatial model maintenance, and publication of model updates. The Vehicle module is responsible for real-time model generation and consistency.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes worksite model updates (terrain grid, obstacle list, hazard positions) to Telemetry interface at ≥2 Hz. Consumers: planning functions, teleoperation UI, safety services.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle receives mission tasking (e.g., 'map area X') from planning/operator; tasking may include region-of-interest or sensor configuration hints.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes detected hazards (personnel, obstacles in work zone) via SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services for real-time risk assessment.

module_containment_scores.notes: Vehicle is the natural owner: it has direct access to sensors, compute, and real-time constraints. SLAM is a core vehicle autonomy function. Minimal diffusion: only model publication crosses module boundary.
Teleoperation is a consumer of the model, not a producer. Placing SLAM in Teleoperation would require remote compute and introduce latency; poor fit for real-time safety-critical mapping.

Safety Services consumes hazard data but does not generate the worksite model. Placing SLAM in Safety Services would decouple it from vehicle sensors and real-time execution; violates separation of concerns.
NGHE Solution is the system-level container; mapping is a vehicle-level function. Assigning to NGHE Solution would be too abstract and would not clarify ownership or interface responsibilities.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Vehicle module is the optimal owner because it has direct access to sensors, onboard compute, and real-time execution context. SLAM is a core autonomy function that must run with low latency and high availability. Publishing model updates via Telemetry interface decouples consumers (planning, teleoperation, safety) from the implementation details of SLAM.
containment_risks: Risk: Vehicle compute may be resource-constrained; SLAM may compete with other autonomy tasks (path planning, obstacle avoidance). Mitigation: define compute budget and prioritization policy.

Risk: Model updates published via Telemetry may exceed network bandwidth in high-fidelity scenarios. Mitigation: implement adaptive compression and selective transmission (e.g., only changed regions).
Risk: SLAM failure (e.g., in featureless terrain) may leave Vehicle without a valid model. Mitigation: implement fallback to dead-reckoning and operator-assisted mapping.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify Vehicle module's compute budget allocation for SLAM. Define Telemetry message format and compression scheme. Specify SLAM algorithm and sensor requirements. Document fallback behavior and operator override mechanisms.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (centralized SLAM on vehicle) directly addresses the refined FR: it generates a real-time worksite model from onboard sensors and publishes it to planning/execution functions. The DP is specific, implementable, and traceable to the FR. No logical gaps or contradictions.

diffusion_risks: Model publication via Telemetry crosses module boundary; bandwidth and latency must be managed.
SLAM algorithm and compute platform must be specified to avoid ambiguity in Vehicle module scope.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Vehicle module is the clear owner of SLAM and sensor fusion. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimal. No diffusion of core mapping logic across modules.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: model fidelity can be measured against ground truth (survey data, manual inspection); update rate and latency can be instrumented; interface compliance can be tested via integration tests.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Vehicle owns SLAM and model generation; Telemetry interface carries model updates; Safety Services and Teleoperation consume the model. Ownership and interface responsibilities are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Specify SLAM algorithm (e.g., ORB-SLAM2, LiDAR-based ICP) and compute platform (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson AGX) in DP detail.
Define Telemetry message schema for worksite model (e.g., octree, voxel grid, point cloud format).
Specify model update rate (e.g., 10 Hz) and compression strategy (e.g., delta encoding, selective transmission).
Document fallback behavior if SLAM tracking is lost (e.g., dead-reckoning, operator-assisted mapping).
Define SafetyAlert message format for hazard publication (e.g., hazard type, position, confidence).

decision.justification: The refined FR is decomposable into five sub-FRs (sensor acquisition, data fusion, classification, model maintenance, interface exposure). Each sub-FR is a distinct functional concern that requires further design detail. DP-A is sound and well-allocated to Vehicle, but the sub-FRs must be decomposed to the next level to specify sensor types, fusion algorithms, classification methods, and interface protocols. High safety criticality demands detailed specification of hazard detection and representation.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain and obstacle data from onboard sensors (LiDAR, camera, IMU).
CR-NGHE-006.2: Fuse multi-sensor data into a unified 3D spatial representation.
CR-NGHE-006.3: Classify and label terrain, static obstacles, and dynamic hazards.
CR-NGHE-006.4: Maintain and update worksite model in real-time during mission execution.
CR-NGHE-006.5: Expose worksite model to planning, teleoperation, and safety services via standardized query/subscription interface.

assumptions: Vehicle compute has sufficient CPU/GPU for real-time SLAM (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson or equivalent).
LiDAR and camera are calibrated and mounted with known extrinsics.
Telemetry interface has sufficient bandwidth for model updates (≥2 Hz, compressed).
SLAM algorithm can initialize and track in typical worksite environments (terrain, vegetation, structures).
Planning and teleoperation functions can consume model updates asynchronously via Telemetry interface.
open_risks: SLAM may fail in featureless terrain or heavy dust/rain; fallback strategy required.
Telemetry bandwidth may be insufficient for high-resolution model updates; compression or selective transmission needed.
Model initialization (first-time mapping) may take 30–60 seconds; mission planning must account for this latency.
Compute resource contention between SLAM and other vehicle autonomy tasks; prioritization policy needed.
Safety-critical hazard detection (e.g., personnel) may require real-time validation; confidence thresholds and operator override mechanisms needed.
evidence_needed: SLAM algorithm selection and performance benchmarks (accuracy, latency, compute cost).
Sensor specifications (LiDAR range/resolution, camera FOV/resolution, IMU accuracy).
Telemetry interface bandwidth and latency measurements.
Worksite environment characterization (terrain types, obstacle density, visibility conditions).
Hazard detection and classification performance (precision, recall, false positive rate).
Integration test results: model generation, update rate, interface compliance.
Safety analysis: FMEA for SLAM failure modes, hazard detection gaps, operator override procedures.
